title: Negative versus positive

stances.claim: Negatives Ã¢â‚¬â€� what we are against Ã¢â‚¬â€� are not enough; positives Ã¢â‚¬â€� what we are for and how we achieve them Ã¢â‚¬â€� are indispensable. The Bill of Rights, the Ten Commandments, and electoral mantras (Marcos's 'Alis diyan!' against Macapagal; Cory's 'Tama na! Sobra na! Palitan na!' against Marcos) are mostly negative, and they fail to deliver fulfillment without sustainable philosophies, implementable programs, and realistic timelines.
stances.rhetorical_move: comparative-formulation analysis across Bill of Rights, Decalogue, and electoral mantras (negative-formulation taxonomy)

stances.claim: The Marxist slogan 'From each according to his ability and to each according to his need' was appealing in theory but appalling in practice for the masses under communist regimes Ã¢â‚¬â€� a formative pre-law lesson that taught CJ that theory must be tested against practice and that slogans need backing philosophies and programs.
stances.rhetorical_move: autobiographical-formation pivot (using a pre-law professor's challenge as origin story)

stances.claim: Despite a happy SC tenure, CJ felt a yearning that he had not done enough because most of his decisions and orders were negative in language Ã¢â‚¬â€� commanding parties to stop irregular behavior, dismissing petitions, or enforcing negatively-phrased constitutional rights Ã¢â‚¬â€� with only a few on the Constitution's positive provisions.
stances.rhetorical_move: self-critique of judicial-output formulation (acknowledging a negative-language tilt in his own decisions)

stances.claim: The FLP, organized on October 13, 2011 with 'dear friends' after tycoons invited CJ to join their companies and shared their yearnings to set aside corporate earnings for philanthropy and CSR, has during its first decade (2011-2021) achieved sustainability, purchased an office condo HQ in Makati, and sponsored four projects (professorial chairs, legal scholarships, dissertation-writing contests, educational assistance for the poor) Ã¢â‚¬â€� funded by over P164 million in donations from family, partners, and friends.
stances.rhetorical_move: institutional-history-as-positive-fulfillment (concrete projects + donor numbers as proof that positives can be implemented)

stances.claim: Through its projects, 'the safeguarding of liberty has been transformed from a mere dream to a sustainable reality' because the professors, scholars, dissertation winners, and education-assisted poor will continue and expand the philosophy 'beyond my lifetime' Ã¢â‚¬â€� with the planned Interactive Museum of Liberty and Prosperity (in partnership with the Supreme Court) as the ultimate first-decade liberty-side project.
stances.rhetorical_move: legacy-continuity framing (positing the project beneficiaries as the philosophy's living continuation)

stances.claim: During its second decade (2021-2031), the FLP will enlarge its focus to help eliminate extreme poverty Ã¢â‚¬â€� initially by sponsoring scholarships and dissertation-writing contests among MBA students majoring in economics, sustainability, business law, and entrepreneurship Ã¢â‚¬â€� moving from the liberty side to the prosperity side of the overarching philosophy.
stances.rhetorical_move: decade-pivot agenda-setting (extreme-poverty elimination as second-decade thesis)

notable_anecdotes.summary: CJ's pre-law political science professor's recurring challenge to refute the Marxist slogan 'From each according to his ability and to each according to his need' Ã¢â‚¬â€� 'difficult to refute' for a 'dirt-poor student like me' because it 'seemed to fulfill my longings and needs' Ã¢â‚¬â€� the most concentrated single-paragraph autobiographical origin story for CJ's eventual liberty-and-prosperity-under-the-rule-of-law philosophy.

The negative-formulation tour across Bill of Rights ('No person shall be deprivedÃ¢â‚¬Â¦'), Ten Commandments ('Thou shall not kill,' 'Thou shall not steal'), and electoral mantras (Marcos's 'Alis diyan!,' Cory's 'Tama na! Sobra na! Palitan na!') Ã¢â‚¬â€� one of CJ's most ambitious cross-domain rhetorical-formulation analyses.

CJ's confessional self-critique that his SC decisions were 'mostlyÃ¢â‚¬Â¦ negative in language' with 'only a few decisions on the positive provisions' Ã¢â‚¬â€� a rare acknowledgment that the institutional form of judging itself tilts negative.

The P164 million figure for first-decade FLP donations Ã¢â‚¬â€� preserved as the column's institutional-scale anchor, with the parenthetical regret that 'due to lack of space, I cannot list our many partners and friends.'

The planned Interactive Museum of Liberty and Prosperity 'to be built in partnership with the Supreme Court' as the ultimate first-decade liberty-side project Ã¢â‚¬â€� an institutional vision CJ would return to in later columns.

The decade-pivot to 'help eliminate extreme poverty' via MBA-economics/sustainability/business-law/entrepreneurship scholarships Ã¢â‚¬â€� making the column the FLP's official second-decade thesis statement.

The closing emphasis on legacy 'beyond my lifetime' Ã¢â‚¬â€� among CJ's most explicit single-line legacy framings, anchored not in personal achievement but in the propagation of the philosophy through the project beneficiaries.

decision_framework_signals: treats negative vs positive formulation as a cross-domain analytical question (constitutional, religious, electoral)
applies theory-vs-practice test to political slogans (Marxism appealing in theory, appalling in practice)
uses 'sustainable philosophies, implementable programs, realistic timelines' as the three-part backing-test for slogans
deploys autobiographical-formation as the origin of philosophy (pre-law professor challenge)
treats own judicial output as predominantly negative-language Ã¢â‚¬â€� a self-critique that motivates post-retirement positive-philanthropy work
frames philanthropy/CSR as institutional implementation of constitutional positives
uses decade-pivot (first decade liberty side Ã¢â€ â€™ second decade prosperity side) as institutional thesis structure
anchors legacy in beneficiary-propagation 'beyond my lifetime' rather than in personal achievement

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban marks the Foundation for Liberty and Prosperity's 10th anniversary by reflecting on the FLP's four October themes Ã¢â‚¬â€� theory versus practice, dreams versus realities, negative versus positive, slogans versus philosophies. He recalls his pre-law political science professor challenging him to refute the Marxist slogan 'From each according to his ability and to each according to his need,' a slogan 'difficult to refute' for a 'dirt-poor student' like him; yet he knew that communism, however appealing in theory, was appalling in practice, leading him later to forge his legal philosophy of 'safeguarding liberty and nurturing prosperity under the rule of law.' He observes that the Bill of Rights is written mainly in negative language ('No person shall be deprived of life, liberty or property without due process of lawÃ¢â‚¬Â¦'), as are most of the Ten Commandments ('Thou shall not kill,' 'Thou shall not steal') and electoral mantras (Marcos's 'Alis diyan!' against Macapagal; Cory's 'Tama na! Sobra na! Palitan na!' against Marcos). Constitutional positives Ã¢â‚¬â€� like 'The goals of the national economy are a more equitable distribution of opportunities, income, and wealth' Ã¢â‚¬â€� and Jesus's two positive commands ('Love God with all your heartÃ¢â‚¬Â¦ and love your neighbor as I love you') exist but are outnumbered, and slogans rarely transform into fulfillment without 'sustainable philosophies, implementable programs, and realistic timelines.' He confesses that even his SC decisions were mostly negative in language Ã¢â‚¬â€� commanding stops, dismissing petitions, enforcing negatively-phrased rights Ã¢â‚¬â€� with only a few on positive provisions, leaving a yearning that he had not done enough. After retirement, invitations from tycoons to serve as independent director or adviser revealed their own yearnings for philanthropy and CSR, and together with dear friends he organized the FLP on October 13, 2011. Its first decade (2011-2021) achieved sustainability, purchased an office condo HQ in Makati, and sponsored four projects (professorial chairs, legal scholarships, dissertation-writing contests, educational assistance for the poor), funded by over P164 million from family, partners, and friends Ã¢â‚¬â€� transforming 'the safeguarding of libertyÃ¢â‚¬Â¦ from a mere dream to a sustainable reality' that will continue 'beyond my lifetime,' with the planned Interactive Museum of Liberty and Prosperity (in partnership with the Supreme Court) as the ultimate first-decade project. During its second decade (2021-2031), the FLP will enlarge its focus to help eliminate extreme poverty, initially by sponsoring scholarships and dissertation contests among MBA students in economics, sustainability, business law, and entrepreneurship Ã¢â‚¬â€� pivoting from the liberty side to the prosperity side of the overarching philosophy.
